column1: How big is your family.
column2: Our family is quite large. I have got a younger sister and an elder half-brother. My sister is a student than me and we get on very well.  My half-brother lives a happy family life with his wife and children. I have also got an elder half-sister but I don't know her.

column1: Who do you live with?
column2: I am single and I live with my parents and my younger sister. My father is breadwinner, he works as an ambulance. My mother is at home, she deals with agriculture. This is our family business.

column1: What is your relationship with them like?
column2: I get on very well with my parents and my sister. We sometimes argue about unimportant things but it's natural bacause in every relationship has got small problems. My sister and I share our problems and help each other.

column1: Are you in close contact with those members of your immediate family who don't live with you?
column2: My half-brother lives with his family and he is a very busy person and we don't often see him. Occasionally, we have lunch with them at the weekend.

column1: How important to you is your extended family?
column2: I have very strong family ties. My home and my immediate family is significant to me, and my extended family is as important. I like to see them as often as I can.

column1: Who do you take after?
column2: As for my appearance, I think I take after my mother. When my mother was young, she had long brown hair and she was slightly-built, like me now.

column1: What do you look like?
column2: I am in my late teens. I'm quite tall and I'm slightly-built, but I have rather wide shoulders. I think I am just the right weight. I have wavy long brown hair. My eyes are blue and I wear glasses. If necessary, I apply make-up to cover up my acnes and accentuate my eyes and my mouth.

column1: Do you think looks matter a lot?
column2: Yes, quite a lot. I don't think anyone really wants to look unkempt and unattractive. I admire those who have good taste in clothes but if someone doesn't. This is not problem. After all I believe that one shouldn't judge a book by its cover.

column1: What clothes do you like wearing?
column2: The dressing is important to me, but I don't dress according to the fashion. I wear what I like and fits good. I like to look pretty. I usually wear comfortable clothes at home, but when I go out, I wear my current favorite piece of cloth.

column1: What does your mother/father/ any member of your family look like?
column2: My mother is medium-hight. Her hair is wavy grey but she dyes her brown. Her eyes are also brown. My dad is just as tall as my mom. My dad also has gray hair, but he doesn't dye it. He has a beard and mustache.

column1: What kind of person are you?
column2: I am quite sensitive to what is happening around me. I'm often moody. At the same time I am really understanding, co-operative and generous.

column1: Have you got your father's or mother's personality?
column2: I think I have got my father's personality rather than my mother's personality.

column1: In what respect are you like either of them?
column2: My father is understanding and patient with people. He is a real handyman. He knows a lot of things and he can cope with any job around the house. He is very helpful and practical, but sometimes he loses her temper, just like me.

column1: What is your picture of an ideal husband/wife, mother/father like?
column2: An ideal spouse should be kind and understanding and supportive of his or her partner. Husbands and wives should share household responsibilities so that neither one has to take on more of the burden of running household. In the past, the traditional ideal was that the husband would be the main breadwinner and the wife would stay home, manage the house and raise children. However, nowadays both partners usually need to work. An ideal parent should be responsible and set a good example for his or her children. A parent will have to be patient as children sometimes misbehave or make mistakes as they are growing up. But a good parent is also firm so that children learn good manners and good behavior. Parents should be loving and kind, and both mothers and fathers should play an equal role in their children's upbringing.

column1: How can you prove your identity?
column2: You can prove your identity by showing your identity card, residence card, or perhaps your student card if you have one. When you are outside the Europe Union, you can use your passport for this purpose.

column1: What data can be seen on your identity card?
column2: First of all you can see the holders surname, given name and sex (that is whether the holder is male or female). Then comes the data of expiry. On the other side you can find the holders maiden name and nationality. After this is the mother's name followed by the place, the year, month and day of the holder's birth.

column1: Why do you need to have an ID?
column2: It is best to carry your ID on your wherever you go. You may quite often find yourself in a situation when you have to prove your identity. For example, in court, in exams or when you are stopped by the police.

column1: What happens if you lose your ID?
column2: If you lose your ID, you have to go to the passport office where you are given a form to fill in. You may be asked about your personal particular such as place and date of birth, temporary and permanent address, marital status, family name, first name, maiden name, occupation and place of work. You might also need to take your birth certificate with you. You don't have to worry about your photo as you can have it taken on the spot.

column1: What would you include in your curriculum vitae in order to get the fellowship abroad?
column2: I would write a profile saying: I was born in Szeged. I took my school-leaving exams at Kossuth Zsuzsanna secondary school in Szeged. Meanwhile, I took an evening course in English. I can speak two languages, English and German, fluently. My special field of interest is Webdesign and for the last few years, I have been working for a software development firm. I have a good working knowledge of Photoshop, Adobe Illustrator, Excel, and Word programs. I am quite hard-working and I have a cheerful temperament. I think I can cope with new situations well.

column1: What schools did you attend as a child?
column2: I started nursery school at the age of four, then I went to  elementary school. Later, I was admitted to a high school, where I successfully graduated. Now I want to applying for an university. That's why I'm standing here now.

column1: Did you do well at school.
column2: I wasn't the best student in elementary school, but when I started high school, I became a very good student. And now I'm going to university. So I am staisfied with myself.

column1: Where did you go continue your studies after leaving primary school?
column2: I apply for admission to one of the high school in the country where I spent four years. In the third year I would liked to opted for additional lessons in English and History but that was not possible. I graduated and skipped a year to think about how to continue. I spent the next year studying and decided to go with my studies and apply for university.

column1: Did you enjoy being a student? Why? Why not?
column2: I didn't like going to primary and secondary school. I never had many friends. The class community was not cohesive, the students hurt and bullied each other. However, I still like to study, so I hope that the university will be different. I had a good experience. Most of the teachers always tried to help in their own way. On the other hand, being a student comes with privileges. Thanks to the student card, you can get a discount on many things.

column1: What is the teacher student relationship like in your school?
column2: The teacher-student relationship in my school was never perfect, but most of the students respected their teacher and the teacher treated the students properly. The teacher are all very competent and impart a great deal of knowledge to us.

column1: Which are your favorite subjects?
column2: My favorite subjects were mathematics and history. These were the less popular subjects in my class.

column1: Which are the ones that you don't like?
column2: Physics is not my cup of tea, it is my weak point. I did't even like to think of it. Although I tried to do my best in this subject, my efforts were not always crowned with success.

column1: What are some of the out-of-school activities that you are involved in?
column2: Besides studying, I didn't have time for anything else but, to tell you the truth, there weren't too many opportunities at school. I would have liked to have played basketball, volleyball, or attended an acting class, but these were not possible.

column1: What are your plans for the future?
column2: I don't know yet what I would like to do when I leave school. One thing is sure, I would like to go on to higher education. I am aware of having to study hard and that it won't be easy but it will work.

column1: Do you get up as soon as you wake up?
column2: No, never. In fact, I need some time till I regain my senses and am wide awake again. This usually happens after I have washed my face.

column1: Do you ever oversleep?
column2: Yes, unfortunately it has happened to me sometimes. Being the heavy sleeper, I can hardly hear the alarm clock ring.

column1: What do you do first when you get up?
column2: I draw the curtains to let light into my room. Then I go to the bathroom to wash my face in cold running water that I will be wide awake.

column1: What do you do before breakfast?
column2: I go to the bathroom and wash my face, hands and dry myself with a towel. Then I clean my teeth, comb my hair, and begin to get dress. If necessary, I apply make-up to cover up my acnes and accentuate my eyes and my mouth.

column1: Who gets breakfast ready?
column2: Breakfast for me is just snacks so we don't make much fuss over it.  My family doesn't often have breakfast together, so most of the time everyone prepares breakfast for themselves. So we don't make much fuss over it. However, when we have breakfast together, my mother usually makes the breakfast.

column1: What do you usually have for breakfast?
column2: I start with a yogurt or drink a mug of cocoa. Then I eat a sandwich or some kind of egg or French toast. My mom often buys pastry, so I eat them.

column1: How do you go to work/school?
column2: I go to the bus stop by bicycle, which is 5 minutes away from our house. I get on a bus that drops me off near the school. If I'm in a hurry, I go by bus or tram. Otherwise I go on foot.

column1: How many hours do you work in a day?
column2: I work flexitime because I work from home. I distribute the ratio of work and free time myself. I usually work 6-8 hours.

column1: Where do you have lunch?
column2: I always have lunch at home. I don't think I could ever put up with the kind of meal you get in a canteen. What my mother cooks at home is more to my taste.

column1: When do you go home?
column2: I work and learn at home, so I don't go out much. If I leave home, I get home late in the afternoon.

column1: How do you spend the afternoons?
column2: When I'm not working, I'm learning. If my parents need me around the house, I help them. I spend my free time with my friends.

column1: How do you share the housework in your family?
column2: My mother does most of the housework. She does the cooking, washing, shopping, watering flowers and taking care of the garden and feeding the cats. My sister is enthusiastic about cleaning. She hoovers the carpets and dust the furniture. He has a dog, so he has to take the dog for a walk. My father mows the grass, washes the car, takes care of the farm animals and does all the men's work on the farm. I clean my own room and do any other jobs that need doing.

column1: What jobs should be done every day/at least once a week?
column2: You have to air the rooms and put them in order every day so that they should look clean and tidy. Washing-up is again one of those household chores that needs to be done frequently. Other jobs such as sweeping the floor, emptying the waste-bin, cleaning the windows can be done less frequently but you should do most of them at least once a week.

column1: Is the mother's place in the home?
column2: No, I don't think so. In my opinion, it is selfish not to let a woman build a career. You can't deprive a woman of her right to earn her own living, find pleasure in her work and keep in touch with real life. A mother who is able to build a career is able to provide a suitable environment for her family and appropiate upbringing for her child in addition to work.

column1: Should women who stay at home with the children be paid in the same way as other people are?
column2: There is no way you could pay a housewife. The husband should be paid enough so that he can support his wife and children properly. Unfortunately, this is often not the case.

column1: How many jobs are women responsible for?
column2: If a woman goes out to work, she is responsible for at least three jobs. Some women hold high positions and are good mothers and housewives at the same time.

column1: Can a woman fulfil all the roles of a wife, mother and a career woman?
column2: It depends on the husband's attitude. If he shares the responsibility of bringing up a child and caring for the family with his wife, which should ideally happen in every family, women will be less exhausted  and more efficient both at work and at home. However, in many cases a single woman is also able to do this.

column1: Who wears the trousers in your family?
column2: My father does. Important decisions are always made by him but my parents usually make decisions together.

column1: What houshold jobs are husbands often responsible for?
column2: Men are usually good at repairing household devices. A handyman can fix a dripping tap, replace a burnt-out light bulb, stick on wallpaper, repair a leaking water-pipe.

column1: Who can stay at home on child care leave?
column2: In Hungary, it is either the mother or the father who can stay at home with the new-born baby. In practice, however, it is still the mother who goes on maternity leave for one, two or sometimes three years.

column1: Is it worth staying at home until the child is three years old?
column2: Yes. Definitely. The first few years of a child's life are crucially important. This is the time of creating contact between parent and child. There cannot be anything more important for a mother than attending to a child during the first few years of its life.

column1: How do you spend the evenings at home?
column2: I learn till about 8. After, I skype with my friend. I take a warm bath and go to bed at about 10-11. Until I fall asleep, I watch tv or I relax with some interesting book or a bit of music.

column1: In what way is your daily routine different at the weekends?
column2: At the weekends the days are slightly different. If there is no family program, I learn and help around the house. I try to spend some time outside and meet my friends. We ofen go walking or ride a bike.

column1: Would you like to make any changes in your life-style or are you happy with the way of life you are leading?
column2: I suppose there is always something to change, but at the moment I am satisfied and I am on the right track. I can manage my time quite well, so I can always afford to take a little rest whenever I need it. I do things in order of importance and never put off till tomorrow what I can do today.

column1: What family occasions do you celebrate?
column2: We celebrate birthdays, name days or Christmas, Easter, wedding  anniversaries and mother's day. Unfortunately, we don't observe father's day.

column1: Do the English celebrate name days?
column2: No, they don't. But birthdays are all the more important to them. On  that day they invite their friends and relatives over and give a big party.

column1: How do you celebrate birthdays?
column2: I always buy a present for my father's, mother's or sister's birthday. Sometimes I make the present myself because I like creating. On that day we usually have some relatives coming in the afternoon and wishing a happy birthday. It is a quiet family gathering which is spent on eating, drinking and discussing family matters.

column1: How does a couple get married in Hungary?
column2: People can get married either in a church or in a registry office. In the latter case there must be two witnesses. Most people however, prefer to have a church wedding.

column1: What happens at the wedding reception?
column2: After the wedding ceremony there is a large reception where an enormous meal is eaten, the wedding cake is cut. The guests are dressed in their best clothes and dance to music. Old traditions such as dancing with the bride at midnight and the bride leaving to change clothes after the dance are still kept at most wedding parties.

column1: On what other occasions do the members of the wider family meet?
column2: The members of the wider family meet at Christmas, Easter, birthdays, weddings, funerals.

column1: What is the greatest family festival for you?
column2: I think in this part of the world Christmas is the greatest family festival for everybody. It is celebrated with ancient customs and traditions.

column1: What do we celebrate at Xmas/Easter?
column2: At Christmas we celebrate the birth of Jesus Christ, The Saviour of men.At Easter we celebrate Resurrection of Jesus from the tomb 3 days after his Crucifixion.

column1: What customs and traditions do we celebrate Christmas/Easter with?
column2: Popular customs include advent candle lighting, exchanging gifts, decorating Christmas trees, sharing meals with family and friends. Most Easter traditions are associated with Easter Monday when boys set out to call on their female friends and relatives and sprinkle them with perfume or water. Girls, in return, treat them to home-made cakes or some drinks and present them with eggs painted mainly red, green or yellow. Children are also presented at Easter with either some chocolate eggs or a chocolate Easter bunny.

column1: What happens on Christmas Eve?
column2: December 24th is Christmas Eve. The Christmas tree is decorated and children are given a lot of presents. Most families go to midnight service in church.

column1: What is a traditional Christmas-Day/Easter Sunday meal like?
column2: On Christmas Day the family gathers round the table to take part in the traditional Hungarian Christmas lunch. Two things are essential parts of this meal: stuffed cabbage and poppy-seed cake. The rest of the meal is usually beef soup followed by pork chops or turkey. A typical Easter Sunday breakfast consists of ham and hard-boiled eggs. There are no set dishes that we eat for lunch at Easter.

column1: Do you approve of the idea of buying presents for your loved ones for  Christmas?
column2: Yes, definitely. There is nothing more pleasing than giving the right present to your loved ones. I admit the right present is difficult to find but it is worth taking the trouble. For me, it is important to see the smile and joy that my gift brings.

column1: How do we celebrate New Year's Eve?
column2: On New Year's Eve people sit up till after midnight to see the new year in. At midnight we sing the national anthem and drink champagne. Some people go to parties and to dances others watch TV. We usually stay up until dawn.

column1: How many public holidays do we have and what do we celebrate on these days?
column2: On March 15th we commemorate the Hungarian Revolution and War of Independence of 1848-49. May 1st is a spring holiday when maypoles are put up in gardens. August 20th is the day of King Stephen l. On this day we commemorate the founding of our state almost a thousand years ago. On October 23rd we celebrate the declaration of the Hungarian Republic and commemorate the revolution of 1956.

column1: What is your relationship like with your parents/children?
column2: I am very fond of my family but I don't always get on very well with them. They try to control me too much and quite often they treat me like a child. My friends are given much more freedom while I am always told what is right and what is wrong. They expect me to regard them as friends but at the same time they just give instructions and  hardly ever ask me about my feelings.

column1: How do you bring up your children?
column2: I think I am quite child-centred. I try to be loving and give my child everything I couldn't have when I was young. However, I am a strict parent. I don't want he/she to spoil her/his life.  I admit I insist on punctuality, tidiness and courtesy from my children at the same time I would like my kid to enjoy life and I am determined to support him as long as I live.

column1: Are parents too permissive with their children nowadays?
column2: Yes, they tend to. These kids can easily get out of control and become unmanageable. In fact the spread of juvenile delinquency is largely due to parental laxity. There are many children hanging around in the streets, who will grow into criminals. At the same time, parents often make the mistake of protecting and pampering the child too much, so she/he becomes conceited and selfish. You have to find the golden mean.

column1: Should children have pocket money?
column2: Yes. Why not let them learn how to manage a certain amount of money and how to make it last for a week or a month. The amount should not be too much, though. In fact, I use it as a kind of reward or punishment by giving more than usual or nothing at all depending on the child's behaviour.

column1: What is your attitude to corporal punishment?
column2: Corporal punishment is ineffective. It only frightens the child and makes both the child and the parent feel unhappy. It is also degrading and cruel. It ruins the child's self-respect and does more harm than good.

column1: How do/would you behave if you find/found your child has/had gone out of control?
column2: Dealing with a naughty child is no easy matter. The only wise attitude the parents can adopt towards their children's behaviour is to sit down with them, ask them about their problems and explain to them what is right and what is wrong.

column1: Do/would you give your children everything they want?
column2: It is only natural for parents to try to do so but giving a child everything he/she wants as soon as he wants it means spoiling him. Children, however, must realise that money is earned by hard work. So parents should try and convince them not to want everything that money can buy.

column1: What are the most important things about educating a child?
column2: Youngsters should be taught respect for other people and respect for the law. They should also be taught patriotism and the need for hard work. They must know what is morally right or wrong.

column1: Do you think schools are able to meet these requirements?
column2: Schools don't always give students an overall moral education. Many youngsters are reaching maturity without learning the hard lessons of life. Parents are inclined to leave the child's education up to the school, which it fails to do properly owing to many different factors. Some of these are high pupil-teacher ratios, lack of time and money, negligence of teachers.

column1: Are children overburdened at school?
column2: Yes, in the best secondary schools in Hungary students have to cope with an enormous workload. Teachers force them to do a lot of homework and take oral tests in class, which puts an unrelenting mental pressure on them. Students are required to make an even more intense effort in the third and fourth years of grammar school so that they can get admission to university.

column1: Have you or your friends ever had any experience of the generation gap?
column2: Yes, I suppose everybody has. The generation gap is by no means a modern phenomenon. The young have always reacted against the accepted beliefs of their elders. I myself was a rebel in my early teen days and mostly had a difference of opinion with my father. To tell you the truth, I still don't know why my father was against me going steady with a boy at the age of 16.

column1: What are some of the problems teenagers have?
column2: By the time they are 16 or 17 they almost certainly have experienced some disillusionment with the adult society. They would like to be given unrestricted freedom. They feel that their parents do not understand them, which leads them to ignore parental advice. That's why they often feel frustrated about their own affairs. Teenagers need extra attention both from parents and teachers otherwise they may easily turn to drugs or violence.

column1: How can you get your children to study?
column2: You must trust a child. Give him responsibility and let him decide if he wants to study or not. He will never study if he doesn't want to   even if you are standing behind his back. If nothing works you just tell her/him that he/she'll get a bicycle or a new pair of jeans if he/she studies hard and this always does the trick.

column1: What are the job and career prospects for young people today?
column2: Quite promising, there are far more possibilities today than ever  before. Nevertheless, much harder work, more self-discipline, more constructiveness and a more rational approach are required of them than even some years ago.

column1: How much freedom should children have in the choice of their careers?
column2: Parents should have a say but the final decision should be left up to the child. After all it is his life, he should know what he wants to do with it.

column1: Should parents still support their children once they have grown up?
column2: Yes, I think so. Otherwise newly weds for example, would not be able to set up their own homes and raise two or three children. Only with the help of a grandmother or a grandfather can a mother go out to work or can parents do some entertaining such as going out together. I think a good parent always supports their children. If not with money, then with time.

column1: What are the effects of television on family life?
column2: I think it is an important part of family life. In the evenings, when the whole family is at home, they can spend some time together without much fuss. However, Tv can also cause harm. If a family watches too much TV, they can deprive themselves of important family conversations. Like asking your child what grades he/she got at school or your husband/your wife how his day was.

column1: What are the effects of computers on family life?
column2: You can't avoid using a computer nowadays. Most people regularly  use the computer for writing e-mails, chatting with their friends or for getting information. The problems begin when they become addicted to it and sit in front of the computer all day. After a while, they will not be able to establish real contact with the people around them and they will prefer virtual friends to real ones. They may become alienated from their own family members.

column1: Do you think family life is different today from what it was like 40-50 years ago?
column2: Yes. Now there are fewer opportunities for enjoying the company of other people because everybody is busy and they don't have time for each other. So many electronic devices deprive people of the opportunity to develop personal social relationships. We used to know all of our neighbours but this is no longer the case. We are becoming isolated from each other and, what is even worse, from the members of our own family. Also families used to be much larger. My grandmother was one of six children. Very few people nowadays have such large families.

column1: Are you against or in favour of large families? Why?
column2: I think large families are happy families. The children in a large family naturally learn how to be considerate to others. For them the family is a large unit in which every single member stands by the other whatever happens. They can share their sorrows and they can share their joys. They have the advantage of never feeling lonely. However, it is important to know that you should only have a big family if you are able to support them.

column1: Where do you come from?
column2: I come from a small town in the south of Hungary.

column1: What is the region like?
column2: The ground is mainly flat there. If you go outside the town and look around you, you can see the land stretching away into the distance. It is an impressive landscape. It has quite rich flora. Vast tracts of land and pastures are found here.

column1: What is your town/area famous for?
column2: Our area is basically a paradise for tourists. Lake Balaton, which has a charm of its own, attracts a lot of visitors from year to year. Around the lake new hotels have been built, motels have been opened in the most attractive spots, well-equipped camping sites have been established. The lake is flanked by an almost continuous line of holiday resorts with beaches, sports centres, boat houses, week-end cottages and private summer villas. The country is famous for its delicious real Hungarian food. For example, beef stew, goulash soup, fish soup.

column1: Is it mainly an industrial or an agricultural area?
column2: It is an agricultural area, where nearly half the population works on the land growing crops and raising animals. Farmers use machines and advanced methods to produce the largest possible amount of food. They grow wheat, maize, barley and oats as well as root crops like sugar beet, carrots and turnips. Others raise livestock, mainly pigs and cattle.

column1: What are the old parts of your town like?
column2: My town has a long history, which, consequently, has a lot of monuments. The town experienced many destructions, but most parts were always rebuilt.

column1: Are there any buildings of historic or architectural interest in your area?
column2: Szeged is 20 km from where I live, where there are many buildings of historic and architectural interest. For example, Dom Square, Széchenyi Square, REÖK - Regional Art Centre,  Water Tower, Gate of Heroes

column1: Should old buildings of historic interest be preserved or pulled down?
column2: I think these old buildings of historic interest must be preserved and they should be incorporated into the new surroundings. It is being done really successfully in most of our towns.

column1: Is it a privilege to live in a town or in the capital? Why (not)?
column2: I live in the country and I like it here. But I recognise that there are privileges to city life.

column1: Is your living place far from the capital?
column2: The place where I live is a long way off from the capital, it is a out-of-the-way town.

column1: How many inhabitants are there in your town?
column2: The population of our town is about 6000. The people here are very friendly and are close to nature. I appreciate the clean atmosphere.

column1: What are the differences between life in the country and life in the city?
column2: Life is never dull in a town, the latest exhibitions, films and plays are  within walking distance or only a short bus ride away. There are more civilised conditions in a town,. streets are better kept and shops are better supplied. City life is also better for the children because there is a variety of schools they can choose from and they don't need to commute. Neither do adults need to commute to work because these  places of work are usually within easy reach. It is, in fact, another advantage that people have more chance of employment and thus more chance of success. On the other hand in the town people lose touch with nature, they rush to work in the mornings and come home dead tired in the evenings.

column1: Where would you prefer to live: in a town or in the country?
column2: I live in a country but I would prefer to move to a town because town life is so much more exciting. Towns are constantly changing places and they can provide you with the best that life can offer.

column1: Which do you think is better for your physical and mental well-being?
column2: Nothing can be compared with the sight of green trees and pastures, the sound of twittering birds and the silent nights when the cock crowing is the first sound that disturbs serenity. All this can work wonders for people's mental and physical well-being, while in a town there are crowds, noise and pollution.

column1: What are some of the problems big industrial cities (Budapest) have?
column2: Budapest suffers from industrial decline, unemployment, poverty and housing and social stress. In general the larger a city the higher the degree of air pollution. Even rivers are dirtier in a city than in the country. Public transportation is usually inadequate, buses are crowded and people resort to using their own cars. This, however, leads to another problem: there are constant traffic jams when the cars wait for a longer time than they move forward and their drivers suffer from the noxious fumes. In big cities skyscrapers and high-rise blocks are built right next to each other without leaving space for greenery.

column1: Why are there so many homeless people on the streets?
column2: Homelessness is an acute problem that towns like Budapest have to face. Homeless people occupy many of the waiting rooms at railway  stations but you can bump into them in underpasses. Some have become homeless through divorce, others have failed to keep up mortgage payments and there are perhaps even more who have come as refugees from some of the developing countries.

column1: What are big housing estates like?
column2: Big housing estates are usually situated around industrial centres on the edges of towns. Most families there live in overcrowded and unsatisfactory conditions. There are huge blocks of flats which are faceless and all identical. The flats are usually too small for the families that live in them.

column1: In what way are newly built districts different?
column2: There has been a tendency for the last decade or so to design and build residential areas with more colour and individuality. The huge, faceless blocks of flats are changing into something that is more welcoming and pleasant.

column1: Would you rather live in a big housing estate or in the green belt? Why?
column2: Although in a big housing estate almost each block of flats contains shops and other facilities I would still like to live in the green belt because it is much quieter. In the green belt there are residential areas mainly with semi-detached or detached houses which are more personal and intimate places than high-rise blocks. The trees outside the houses provide you with the fresh air you lack so much in the centre or in big housing estates.

column1: Do you like the town where you live?
column2: I like my town. It is a very small city, but it has a lot to offer. If you have a specific need, Szeged is easily accessible. It lies at a distance of 20 km from my town. Besides, my town is very famous for its spa, so it is known all over the country.

column1: Where do you live: in, near or far from the centre?
column2: I live on a farm on the outskirts of the town but it is quite close from the town centre.

column1: What amenities are there in your area?
column2: The place where I live offers quite a few facilities you may need, good restaurants, parks, playgrounds, an event center, a spa, a theatre, and even some sporting events.

column1: Do you have to go far for your shopping?
column2: I don't have to go far for my shopping. Shopping facilities, including a supermarket, a post office, a chemist's and other amenities are near at hand. Except when I need furniture or electronic devices.

column1: Where do you live: in a house or in a flat?
column2: I live in a detached house with a big garden.

column1: What are the disadvantages of living in a flat?
column2: A block of flats is rather densely populated. Sometimes people don't know even their next-door neighbours. You can often hear them as the walls are not completely soundproof. The flat doesn't have a garden and often doesn't even have a balcony.

column1: What are the advantages of living in a house?
column2: In a house there is more room and more privacy. In a house you are on your own and nobody disturbs you. You can have a garden in which you can grow flowers or sit on the lawn. Your children can also feel safer because they can play in the fenced-off yard.

column1: Which is cheaper?
column2: Since land is becoming more and more expensive, it is cheaper to build high blocks of flats from prefabricated materials than to construct hundreds of small houses. The overheads are also lower if you live in a flat than if you live in a house.

column1: What is the layout of your house/flat like?
column2: If you look at our house from the outside, the front door is in the center. This opens directly into a small room. This is the hall. On the left side find my bedroom. Another door leads to the corridor where there are doors. The toilet and the bathroom are on the right side. There is a room on the lefthand and there is another door at the end of the corridor. The first leads to the dining room with another two small rooms, the kitchen and the pantry. The second leads to the living room. It leads to 2 bedrooms for my sister and my parents. There is also a smaller corridor that leads to the terrace.

column1: How big are the rooms?
column2: The hall is about 3 metres by 2 metres. The toilet and the bathroom are small, each of them is about three and a half square metres. The living room is about twenty square metres while the bedrooms measure about twelve metres square each.

column1: Is the whole flat/house big enough?
column2: It is a 150 square metre house. It is perfect for a family of four.

column1: What type of accommodation is it?
column2: It is a freehold house so we don't need to pay rent.

column1: Is it comfortable?
column2: Our house is fairly comfortable and it is equipped with all modern conveniences such as hot water, electricity, gas heating, and a telephone.

column1: How are the windows situated?
column2: All windows face the garden so that one can have a good view of the garden. We have a light and pleasant living room thanks to the huge windows.

column1: What kind of curtains (blinds, shutters) have you got?
column2: There are full-length, monochrome curtains on the windows. Some windows also have got curtains and blinds which we pull down in the evenings and raise in the mornings.

column1: How is your living-room furnished?
column2: Along the wall opposite the windows, there is a white wall unit with my mother's vases. The focal point of the room is the elegant four-piece suite, consisting of three sofas and one comfortable armchair. The case stands in the corner with the television, next to a lamp. A small coffee table with a glass top is in the center.

column1: What are the walls and the floor like?
column2: The walls are painted in different colors and there is a 15 cm strip of wallpaper around some centimeters from the ceiling. The floor is covered with wood parquet, in some places there also is carpet.

column1: How big is your kitchen?
column2: Our kitchen is of medium size but the space is used very efficiently and we have a dining room.

column1: How is it furnished?
column2: The various items of kitchen furniture are conveniently arranged in a continuous line around the walls. All kitchen equipment is built-in, including an electric cooker, microwave, dishwasher and sink.

column1: Do you cook on a stove / by gas / by oil or by electricity?
column2: We have a gas-cooker but the oven is electric. I think this combination is the best for cooking and baking.

column1: Do you have a refrigerator / deep-freeze?
column2: We have a refrigerator with a small deep freeze but we have also a big freezer.

column1: What are the benefits of having a fridge?
column2: It is good for keeping our food fresh and cool. We also put some of the home-grown vegetables and fruit into the deep freeze for the winter.

column1: What labour-saving devices have you got?
column2: Our kitchen is almost fully fitted with labour saving devices, you can now find most of these in nearly all Hungarian households. In the kitchen we have a cooker, a fridge, a toaster, a food processor, a dishwasher, a mincer, a blender  and a micro-wave oven.

column1: Where do you keep your labour-saving devices?
column2: We keep the cleaning devices in the bathroom for example: .. and the kitchen utensils in the pantry.

column1: How about cupboard space? Is there plenty of it?
column2: There is never enough space in the cupboard as we have a number of small things that we have to place somewhere in the kitchen or in the dining room. On the shelves, there are spices and the ingredients needed every day for cooking like salt, flour, sugar, and cooking oil.

column1: What do you keep in the cupboard?
column2: Saucepans, frying pans, jugs, bowls, mugs, the kitchen scales, the mixer, cutlery and the rolling pin are kept in the kitchen cupboard. The working top of the cupboard has a heat resistant surface, which can easily be wiped clean. We keep coffeemaker here.

column1: What kind of floor covering have you got in the kitchen?
column2: The kitchen floor is tiled because it is easy to keep clean.

column1: Have you got a dining-room?
column2: Yes, we have a dining room.

column1: What is it like?
column2: We have a square-shaped dining room. It is about twice the size of the kitchen.

column1: How is it furnished?
column2: There is nothing else in it except for a sideboard with all the plates and the cutlery and a dining-table with six chairs.

column1: How often do you use it?
column2: We use the dining-room only at the weekends when the whole family  has meals together. When we eat informally we just have our meals at the kitchen table.

column1: What things are there in your bathroom?
column2: We have a large tub in the bathroom and there is hot and cold running water. The washbasin is in front of the tub and over the washbasin, there is a cupboard with a mirror. The wash basin next to, there is a towel rack on the wall and a bath mat on the floor.

column1: How are the walls and the floor covered in the bathroom?
column2: Both the floor and the walls are tiled to prevent the plaster from getting wet through the continual splashing which inevitably occurs when you wash.

column1: What do you keep on the bathroom shelves?
column2: On one of the bathroom shelves, we have toothbrushes, toothpaste, deodorants, sprays, shampoo, bubble baths, and body lotion. On the other, we have detergents and washing powder. There is a separate shelf for clean towels.

column1: Is there always hot water in the bathroom?
column2: We have a boiler in the bathroom, which continually provides us with hot water.

column1: What things and pieces of furniture should a children's room contain?
column2: A children's room should contain a cot and a playpen. If the child is a baby there should also be a changing table with rattles over it. Toys are an important part of a child's room and development. The most common toys are building blocks, soft toys, teddy bears, jigsaw puzzles, dolls and dollhouses, toy soldiers, racing cars, and rocking horses.

column1: How is your bedroom furnished?
column2: My bedroom has a double bed right next to the door. The bedside table, the wardrobe, the bookshelf, and the chest of drawers are put along the wall. My desk is in the corner by the window. On the bed, we have bedspreads with lots of cushions. The floor is covered with parquet, and in some places there are carpets. The curtains on the windows shut out the morning light.

column1: Have you got an attic / cellar / balcony?
column2: We have a rather large attic at the top of the house.

column1: What do you use them for?
column2: We keep old stuff and useless things there.

column1: Do you have a convenient working space or a desk at home?
column2: The children have their own desks in their bedrooms.

column1: Where do you keep your books?
column2: The textbooks are kept on the desk in my bedroom. Otherwise, I keep my other books on the bookshelf in my room.

column1: What kind of furniture do you like?
column2: I like modern furniture, but I also like antique pieces.

column1: What colour scheme do you prefer?
column2: I prefer matt colors to bright ones. I like to have cream-colored sofas and dark brown upholstered armchairs in our sitting room.

column1: Do you like decorated or bare walls?
column2: The walls and the shelves in our house are adorned with various ornaments and I always consider the style of the room when I buy a new piece.

column1: What kinds of ornaments do you like?
column2: I especially like the fairy lights and garlands. I like hand-made decorations and candlesticks and vases. I also have some wood carvings, and ceramics in the living room as well as some images in my bedroom.

column1: Is your flat crammed with things?
column2: Yes, I feel that my house is crammed with things. However big your house is you still feel that you don't have enough room and that you can't find a suitable place for your shoes, overcoats, suitcases or what have you.

column1: What can be done in a flat to make it more spacious?
column2: It needs some built-in cupboards or hideaways or at least one more store room.

column1: How is your flat lit?
column2: Naturally, we had the electricity put in when the house was built. We have stand lamps as well as there are chandeliers in every room. Due to the huge windows, a lot of natural light enters the house.

column1: What kind of switches have you got in your flat?
column2: We have nice white switches on the color walls.

column1: How many sockets are there?
column2: We have a lot of sockets to plug in all the electric equipment and appliances we use in the household.

column1: Have you got a mobile phone / CD player/ DVD player / computer?
column2: All parts of my family have mobile phones. Mobile is a very useful thing if you want to be reached wherever you are. We also have CD and DVD players, but we don't use them. We have two computers and a laptop in our house so we don't need them.

column1: What kind of heating is there?
column2: We have gas heating and we also have a tile stove that can be heated with wood.

column1: How do other people heat their houses?
column2: Quite a few people use convector heaters in Hungary and some  families have electric heaters. Tile stoves and fireplaces have become  popular recently because people like sitting by the blazing fire when it is cold and windy outside.

column1: How often do you redecorate the rooms?
column2: We just do up our house every 5 to 6 years. But I redecorate my room every year.

column1: Do you do your own redecoration and repairing yourself or do you prefer  to have it done for you?
column2: There are certain things that we have matter done but mainly we do ourselves.

column1: What changes have you made in your house since you moved in?
column2: Our house was built by my father's family. Over the years we have constantly renovated something. Shortly after I was born, my parents added two rooms to the house: a living room and a bedroom.  One year ago the kitchen has been completely refurnished. In the last few years we replaced some windows and converted the garage into a room. Now it is my room.

column1: What things can go wrong in a house?
column2: From time to time there are certain things in a house that go wrong and need fixing. For example when the tap keeps dripping, the pipes are leaking or the drain blocked, you can call for the plumber or try to repair them yourself.

column1: How can you have them repaired?
column2: Fortunately, my father can fix all the electrical appliances so we don't have to call the mechanic when there is no colour on the TV,  one of the domestic equipment refuses to work. He can even repair the car, whatever is wrong with it. If necessary, he can quite easily put up a new shelf, drive in a nail, replace a burnt-out bulb. He can also cope with a blocked drain, a burst pipe or a broken wire.

column1: Have you ever been to an old and ugly flat? What was it like?
column2: Once I went to a very old and uncomfortable flat. It was dark inside because it had tiny windows and the air was stuffy. The floor was worn out and in the bathroom there was only rusty bath with a dirty toilet. In the kitchen there was a shabby cupboard and a chipped table. The walls were peeling and the stale air smelt of mice. The only bedroom, which was used as a sittingroom too, was furnished with odds and ends of tatty furniture.

column1: What are old houses like?
column2: Old houses usually have wooden steps and floors which are slowly worn down by people's feet. Tiles fall off the roof and get broken, the paint cracks and peels. Pipes can begin to rust and bricks to crumble if they aren't looked after properly.

column1: Have you ever seen a house being built?
column2: I haven't seen a house being built but my father often expand our house. Last year he built a summer kitchen, not incidentally he formed our garage to a room. This is my bedroom.

column1: What does the bricklayer / carpenter / joiner / tiler / plumber / glazier / electrician / plasterer / painter / decorator do?
column2: The man who builds walls is called the bricklayer. The bricks are stuck together with mortar. The carpenter does the woodwork of the house. He puts strong wooden beams on the top so that they can support the roof. Plumbers lay pipes and fix the guttering and drainpipes. Electricians put in all the wires, lights and sockets. Then the joiner fixes the windowledges and when the walls are plastered he fixes the doors and other woodwork. The tiler fixes the tiles and leaves the house ready for the painters and decorators.

column1: What does a typical English house/flat look like?
column2: An average English family lives in a medium-sized, semi-detached house. The rooms are usually not too big. Often, there are brightly patterned carpets all over the floors and the stairs. The house usually has a small garden in front of it, a small garage for the car at the side and another small garden at the back.

column1: How does an average English family buy its house?
column2: They buy their three-bedroom house with the help of a long-term loan or mortgage from a building society. It is expensive, and when they have finished paying off the mortgage in 20 years' time or so, they will have paid about twice as much as the original cost of the house.

column1: How would you advertise your own house/flat if you wanted to sell it?
column2: Magnificent suburban residence. Two entrance, three double bedrooms, bathroom with huge bath and shower room, internal and external toilets, hall, dining-room, two kitchen, double garage, beautiful mature garden, gas-fired central heating. In first-class condition.

column1: In what types of housing can people live?
column2: People can live in skyscrapers, high-rise blocks, blocks of flats, terraced houses, semi-detached houses, detached houses, bungalows, huts, and cottages. They can own their flats — in that case, they are called freehold flats or they can rent a flat. They can share a flat with other families and live in a co-tenancy. Some people live in lodgings.

column1: Have you ever lived in lodgings?
column2: I have never lived in lodgings but I can imagine that you have to keep a number of house rules.

column1: What were the house rules?
column2: You are not supposed to smoke in the common rooms, you are not allowed to be too noisy, take girls or boys into your room, stick pictures on the wall and you have to use the bathroom at a fixed time.

column1: Where do most students live?
column2: Students who live away from home use student hostels and university halls of residence provided by universities in separate buildings. Lodgings are used by those who can't get a hostel room. This means that they take a bed-sitting room in a private house and pay monthly rent to the landlady.

column1: Is it difficult to get a flat in Hungary?
column2: I wouldn't say it is difficult to get a flat/house if you have the right amount of money. However, it is not very common for families to have a house to themselves with a garden. Property prices are very high so people often have to wait for years before they can have a place of their own.

column1: Is there a shortage of living accommodation in Hungary?
column2: I don't think there is a shortage of living accommodation in Hungary. Rather, the problem is that young married couples don't have enough money even to buy a small flat of their own and so often they have no other choice but to live with their parents.

column1: How can you get a flat in Hungary?
column2: If you have enough money you can buy a flat/house, build a house yourself or have a flat/house built. However, most people cannot afford this. If they don't want to rent a flat/house or live with their parents, they can get a loan from a bank.

column1: How can you get the money for building a house or buying a flat?
column2: Getting a loan is usually difficult, because most banks demand that you have some money in order to be entitled to a mortgage. Once you get the loan, usually at a very high interest rate, you have to pay it back in  monthly instalments for many years. If you have two or more children, you may get a state grant, which you do not have to pay back.

column1: What should be done about the housing problems?
column2: To tackle housing as a high social priority requires spending on a vast scale on public housing. The local authorities should build many blocks of flats and let them to people who can't afford to build or buy a flat of their own. Interest rates should be reduced, run-down houses should be renovated and sold cheaply, all taxes on building materials should be removed and building industries should work harder and more efficiently.

column1: Where are you employed?
column2: I have been working in an shop for 2 years.

column1: What do you do for a living?
column2: I have been employed as a junior clerk.

column1: What is your position?
column2: I am a dress shop worker.

column1: What are your responsibilities?
column2: I have to do a bit of everything. My main job is to manage the cash register and I also help with the clothes sorting and the clothes put in their place.

column1: Do you enjoy working there?
column2: I like working there, but it's not my dream job. This is just a temporary job, as I start university in September.

column1: Is there a good atmosphere among employees?
column2: My colleagues are very kind and helpful. I get on very well with them. I can ask them to stand in for me, If I have a real grievance. It is a nice atmosphere to work in.

column1: How do you get on with your employer?
column2: My boss is all right in every respect, I can't say anything against him.

column1: Do you find your work easy or tiring?
column2: I think it is a tiring job but it is worth doing it.

column1: Have you got good chances of promotion?
column2: If you work hard and honestly, you have got chance of promotion.

column1: Is your salary reasonable for the work you are doing?
column2: Yes, I think the pay is quite reasonable for the work that I am doing.

column1: What are the working conditions like?
column2: We have pleasant and healthy surroundings, with a nice and spacious air-conditioned shop.

column1: How many jobs have you worked in so far?
column2: This is the first job I've had. Unfortunately, I didn't have much choice when I was looking for a job. It isn't easy to find work these days without experience.

column1: What is your job like now?
column2: Now I am a clothes shop worker. I like working with other people and I like my colleagues, surroundings, and the boss. I find my job not only well-paid but also enjoyable.

column1: Have you ever done manual work?
column2: Of course. My parents have their own business. They deal with agriculture and I help them regularly.

column1: Did you like it?
column2: I don't like it very much because it's a very hard job but my parents need me and I like nature and helping them.

column1: How were / are you paid?
column2: I get a fixed salary every month.

column1: Why did you give up your previous jobs?
column2: The jobs I have had were really hard for me and weren't challenging. Therefore, I decided to go to university.

column1: Have you ever worked flexitime?
column2: No, I have never worked flexitime.

column1: Is it better than working fixed hours?
column2: Yes, I think working flexible hours is better than working fixed hours because when you work flexitime you can fit your work in with your private life and sort out your problems during the day.

column1: Have you ever been unemployed?
column2: Fortunately, I have never been unemployed.

column1: What would you write in a letter of application if you applied for a job?
column2: In a letter of application I would give a kind of profile containing information about my qualifications, schooling, exam results, present and previous jobs, work experience, present salary and perhaps I would give the dates when I am free for an interview.

column1: Have you changed jobs recently? Why? Why not?
column2: No, I haven't changed jobs because it's hard to find a new job without experience these days and I like my current job. Anyway, I will be going to university from September.

column1: Do you think people like to change jobs? Why?
column2: People are unwilling to change their jobs these days even if they have many problems at work. They like to be on the safe side, therefore, they'd much rather stay than leave.

column1: How can you change your job?
column2: If you want to change your job, you must look at the advertisements or make enquiries at companies, firms, factories, offices via Email or post.

column1: Where can you find job advertisements?
column2: You can find job advertisements in the newspapers, magazines or on the Internet.

column1: Who can help you to find the job you are looking for?
column2: If you can't find the right job for yourself in the newspapers, or on the Internet, there are career offices or employment agencies that may still help you and offer you the kind of job you are looking for.

column1: Is it easy to find a job nowadays?
column2: It is not very likely to easily find a job today when unemployment is growing.

column1: What happens if you have found the right job?
column2: If you are lucky and you think you have found the right job, you send your curriculum vitae with your qualifications and details of your previous jobs and go for an interview.

column1: Who talks to the applicant at a job interview?
column2: At institutions, it is usually the headmaster, at companies, it is often the personnel manager who interviews the applicants.

column1: When did you last go to a job interview?
column2: I last went to an interview before I took up my present job.

column1: What did you agree on?
column2: The personnel officer and I agreed on my starting salary, the office hours and the lunch break, and the amount of paid holiday, would be entitled to.

column1: Were you informed about your responsibilities?
column2: I was shown round the shop and told what my responsibilities would be. I was also informed that there were good prospects of advancement and that promotion would depend entirely on my ability and hard work.

column1: Did you accept the job?
column2: As the job and the conditions appealed to me I accepted that I would be on probation for the first three months.

column1: Were you accepted for the job?
column2: Yes, I was accepted for the job. We agreed that if my probationary period was satisfactory I would be taken on the permanent staff.

column1: What do you consider the most important thing about a job?
column2: The most important thing about a job is whether you like it or not. I think I can only make a success of my job if I am interested in it. So it is important to know that you are doing the right job.

column1: What would you say is less important?
column2: It is an important issue as to how you can get on with your employer and with your colleagues, in other words, what the atmosphere is like at work. For me, working conditions and chances for promotion also matter.

column1: How important is money to you?
column2: Of course, you should be adequately paid for your work so that you don't have to work on the side. So I suppose money is quite important for everyone.

column1: Does your present job meet your requirements?
column2: My job does not really meet the requirements I used to have as a youngster. It doesn't use my abilities or stretch my brain.

column1: What do/don't you like about it?
column2: I do not like about my job that it is monotonous and that there is not much rest.

column1: Would you like to retire from your present job or are you considering changing your job?
column2: No, In September I'm going to university and I will not be working.

column1: Do you work full-time or part-time?
column2: I work a part-time job.

column1: What time do you start and finish work every day?
column2: I start work at the shop at eight in the morning.  At 12 o'clock I have a lunch break. I finish at 14 o'clock.

column1: Do you have any breaks during the day?
column2: I have a short break to have my breakfast at twelve. I buy a snack in the canteen, which keeps me alive until the end of the job.

column1: How do you normally spend the day at work?
column2: All day I stand behind the cash register or sort clothes.

column1: How can jobs be grouped?
column2: Jobs can be grouped in several ways for example, manual and nonmanual jobs, skilled and unskilled work or trades and professions.

column1: Who are the blue-collar and white-collar workers?
column2: The workers doing manual jobs are often referred to as blue-collar workers. The ones doing mainly brainwork are called white-collar workers.

column1: What is the difference between jobs and professions?
column2: Some jobs need university qualifications, and these are professions, others don't, and these are skilled, semi-skilled or unskilled.

column1: Name some skilled and unskilled jobs?
column2: Skilled people are those who have learnt a trade such as a pastry-cook, turner, weaver, shop assistant, car mechanic, railwayman, tailor, barber. Unskilled jobs, such as the job of a factory hand or an office cleaner, do not require special training.

column1: When and how do people in different jobs get paid?
column2: Most professional and clerical workers receive a monthly salary. They get their salary slips together with the pay on one of the first five days of every month. Manual workers receive a wage, half of which is paid out in the first, the other half in the second part of the month.

column1: Can lawyers or doctors charge anything for their services?
column2: Certain professional people who are completely or partly selfemployed charge fees for their services, e. g. members of the legal profession (lawyers, solicitors, barristers), and those of the medical profession (general practitioners, dentists, psychiatrists, neurologists, gynaecologists, etc.) who are in private practice.

column1: What do manual workers' earnings comprise?
column2: Manual workers' earnings may comprise several elements such as the basic wage (basic hourly rate), overtime, premium payments for shiftwork, and bonuses (piecework, production or efficiency bonuses, for instance).

column1: Are women equally paid with men?
column2: I think women get equal pay with men in most professions but in a few case the situation is worse.

column1: What do we mean by gross salary and net salary?
column2: The amount of money I actually receive are my net wages or net salary. It is, of course, considerably less than my gross salary, the amount of which is written at the top of my salary slip.

column1: Why is there so much difference between them?
column2: The reason for this is that various deductions are made, e.g. income tax and national insurance.

column1: What does the percentage of the income tax depend on?
column2: The percentage of the income tax depends on the total amount of your annual income.

column1: What other deductions are made from your salary?
column2: Other deductions are the superannuation contributions, which is ten percent of your salary and the unemployment contribution, which is one percent.

column1: Which are the best-paid jobs in today's Hungary?
column2: It seems to me that in today's Hungary bank managers and businessmen, especially if they work in the private industry earn the most.

column1: What occupation would you recommend to your son or daughter Why?
column2: I would recommend my son to set up his own business in the computer science industry, or in any other industry that appeals to him, because I think that this has a future.

column1: What do you think of privatisation?
column2: I think that the government is making a big mistake by selling Hungarian assets to foreign enterprises. We should save our values and retain what is really Hungarian. We should not let the country fall into the hands of foreigners.

column1: What are the advantages and disadvantages of having a lot of multinational companies in Hungary?
column2: I suppose that multinational companies bring variety and a wide range of choice to the life of the country. Without them we would not be able to develop. They are the ones that offer jobs to most Hungarians, thus reduce the level of unemployment.

column1: Why is it difficult to start your own business?
column2: The reason why it is difficult to start a business is two-fold. First of all, you have to have some capital in order to begin. If you don't have money, you can turn to a bank for a loan, which you have to pay back with high interest rates after a certain period of time. But money is still not enough. The most nerve-racking thing in connection with starting your own business is red tape. You have to have a lot of documents and permits so as to start your own company.

column1: What opportunities do you have in the EU?
column2: To the best of my knowledge, since our accession to the European Union in 2004 there has been a free flow of work-force between Hungary and the other countries in the EU. As EU citizens, Hungarians are no longer required to obtain a work permit in order to take up a job in the other EU countries.

column1: Would you like to work in Hungary or abroad?
column2: After graduation I would like to look for a good job in Hungary, because this is the place where I was born and bred. I am not saying that would reject an offer from a foreign company, but I would not be able to work outside my country for good.

column1: What qualities do you need to be a good teacher / driver / nurse?
column2: A good teacher has to be strict and consistent. He should not be on very friendly terms with the children but he should not keep them at a distance either. He has to like the children with whom he is working. Patience on his part is as important as kindness. And also, he has to know his subject well.A driver needs good reflexes and a sure hand. Driving needs concentration and it can be fatal if a driver doesn't attend for even as short a period as a second. He must know the vehicle he is driving inside out and must be able to repair it if it should break down on the road.A nurse should be kind and attentive. She should say some encouraging words to every patient so that they should not be frightened too much by their illnesses. A nurse should also be patient and keep her cool in emergency situations. No nurse should be squeamish or easily disgusted by any sight.

column1: Do you think people have enough money to put aside and save up?
column2: Most people can't afford to put any money aside for their income is so low that it is just about enough to keep body and soul together.

column1: When do people start saving?
column2: People begin to save when there is enough money for all that is needed in the family. But it is more common for people today to ask for bank loans than save up.

column1: What do people save for in Hungary today?
column2: Most people save for a flat / house, a plot of land, a week-end cottage or for holidays abroad. There are some who save for a car or put money aside to purchase durable consumer goods or new furniture. Naturally, many people save without any definite end in view, just for the sake of having a reserve and feeling secure.

column1: Why can't many people afford to put money aside?
column2: Many people can't afford to put any money aside for their income is so low that it is just about enough to keep body and soul together.

column1: How many unemployed are there in this country?
column2: I am afraid I don't really know exact figures. All I know is that at the moment unemployment is still rising.

column1: How do you think the unemployed feel?
column2: The unemployed may feel guilty about being out of work even if it is not their fault. They are disappointed because they receive just enough money not to starve.

column1: What is the unemployment benefit enough for?
column2: I suppose the unemployment benefit is just about enough to cover their basic needs.

column1: What different kinds of trade unions are there?
column2: A large number of unions of various kinds exist in Hungary. The oldest one is the National Association of Hungarian Trade Unions, which organizes workers on the basis of the branch of industry in which they work. There are craft unions, which organize the workers in a specific trade, such as the Democratic Trade Union of Pedagogues.

column1: What do trade unions struggle for?
column2: I think that trade unions struggle for higher pay and better terms of employment and working conditions.

column1: What fringe benefits do you get from your company?
column2: You get a 20% discount in the shop.

column1: How many days of paid holiday do you get?
column2: Most workers receive three weeks' annual holiday with pay but it also depends on the number of years they have spent working. The longer period you have worked the more days of paid holiday you get. The increase is one day per year.

column1: What is the retirement age in Hungary?
column2: The retirement age in Hungary is 65 for those people who were born in 1957 or later than that. There are some occupations from which people can get pensioned off at an earlier age if they have been in employment for at least 40 years. As far as I know one such job is that of a teacher, for example.

column1: How many full meals do you eat a day?
column2: I usually eat three meals, breakfast, lunch and supper. Supper is usually a cold meal for which I don't cook anything.

column1: How many meals are served in an English home?
column2: As far as I know, in an English home four meals are served, breakfast, lunch, tea and dinner.

column1: Which is the most important meal of the day?
column2: Dinner is the most important meal of the day and they serve hot dishes for it.

column1: Why do we say that the English have a large breakfast?
column2: Because an English breakfast consists of cornflakes or porridge, bacon and eggs or ham and eggs (boiled or fried) with baked beans, tomato or mushrooms, buttered toast or bread and butter with marmalade. Instead of bacon and eggs, fish may be served. Some orange juice is drunk before breakfast and either tea or coffee (white or black) is drunk after it.

column1: How do Continental and English breakfasts differ?
column2: A continental breakfast is, of course, much less substantial. We just have some buttered rolls with honey or marmalade or perhaps some sausages or eggs. We don't make much fuss about breakfast. Some people just eat an apple or a banana or drink a cup of tea or coffee.

column1: Is it healthy to have a substantial breakfast?
column2: It is a contentious question to which experts give different answers. Having a substantial breakfast used to be considered healthy but according to the latest findings, we should not have anything but a piece of fruit or a glass of fruit juice for breakfast. As for me, I think you eat when you are hungry, but don't force the food down when you are not.

column1: Who prepares the breakfast in your family?
column2: It is either my mother or me who prepares the breakfast, depending on who has more time.

column1: What do you usually have for breakfast?
column2: We usually eat some cold cuts with rolls, green pepper or tomato and drink tea with lemon, cold milk or hot chocolate. When I am in a hurry, I just drink a cup of strong black coffee.

column1: What do you eat on Sunday morning?
column2: On Sunday mornings we have a more relaxed breakfast, we often eat ham, sausages, bacon and eggs or scrambled eggs, or hard-boiled eggs.

column1: How do you make tea?
column2: To make tea I fill it up with water in the tank of the tea-kettle. Then I put some of the lemon juice in the kettle and teabags in the holder. The water flows through the filter and teabags directly into the can. I serve it with sugar.

column1: What is noise pollution and what can it be caused by?
column2: Noise pollution refers to any loud sounds that are either harmful or annoying to humans and animals. Noise pollution can be generated by airplanes, helicopters, and motor vehicles as well as human activities such as sporting events, concerts, construction, or demolition.

column1: How does noise pollution affect humans and animals in your region?
column2: I live in a small town where noise pollution is not disruptive to humans. In the big cities, it is mainly caused by heavy traffic, constant road works, and frequent open-air concerts and festivals. The schools are located on a very busy avenue and the noise of the traffic and roadworks often impedes concentration and causes mental stress even when the windows are closed. In spring and autumn, students suffer from the heat in the classrooms because opening the windows is unimaginable. Noise pollution also affects animals here. Students can hardly see birds because noise pollution drives them out of the city by decreasing their ability to hear prey or predators.

column1: What is light pollution?
column2: Light pollution is the brightening of the night sky by the use of improper lighting, especially by cities that run lights all night long. It can be caused by extra, unnecessary lights around the home or street lamps that shine light in all directions, instead of pointing light downwards towards the street.

column1: What are the negative consequences of light pollution?
column2: Light pollution not only inhibits the visibility of stars and planets but it may also affect human health and sleep cycles. Excessive light on the retina causes discomfort to the eyes especially during the night. Bright light strains the eyes and also causes headaches and migraine. Light pollution is also a waste of energy because street lamps generally use more energy by shining more light up instead of down. In this way brighter bulbs are needed for the same amount of light.

column1: Is there light pollution where you live? If yes, what is it caused by? If not, can you think of some famous cities? How are they affected by light pollution?
column2: My town is not especially affected by light pollution but unfortunately an increasing number of the world's leading cities want to have a unique identity by lighting important landmarks to create inspiring visual experiences

column1: What is visual pollution?
column2: Visual pollution is what we call anything unattractive or visually damaging to the nearby landscape. Visual environmental pollution can be annoying, ugly or even depressing.

column1: Is there visual pollution where you live?
column2: Visual pollution is not such a big problem in my town. Although there are some abandoned houses and junkyards, there are no skyscrapers that block the natural view. Graffiti or carving on trees, rocks, or other natural features is not as frequent here as in big cities or tourist resorts. However, I am strongly against billboards, which should be banned in cities, for many reasons. Especially before general and local elections they are erected all over the town, many of them illegally, and it takes a lot of time to have them removed.

column1: What substance addictions have you heard of?
column2: Smoking, drinking alcohol, taking drugs and compulsive eating are the most common substance addictions.

column1: How and why does smoking develop in most cases?
column2: Smokers often start the habit in their teens when the urge to fit in is enormous. Teenagers don't want to be viewed as a weirdo who won't try new things that friends, classmates or role-models are doing. Smoking is also seen as a risky behaviour, which is very appealing to many young people who want to challenge parental authority and boring school rules. Many people use the cigarette as a kind of medicine or consolation. They believe that smoking helps them become calmer or concentrate better.

column1: What do you know about the regulation of smoking and its effects?
column2: Despite the fact that tobacco causes serious chronic diseases to both smokers and passive smokers and it is responsible for the death of millions each year all over the world, smoking is still widespread. Fortunately, the number of smokers has been decreasing since smoking was made illegal in public places, including offices, nightclubs, restaurants and pubs. It also helps that cigarettes can no longer be advertised.

column1: Why do many people abuse alcohol?
column2: Alcoholism is a disease that affects people from all walks of life. Psychological, genetic, and behavioral factors can all contribute to having the disease. Alcohol addiction can easily develop, since alcohol is widely available and accepted in western societies. It's often at the center of social situations and closely linked to celebrations and enjoyment. Moreover, many people value drinking alcohol, because it helps to alleviate their negative feelings and removes, at least temporarily, the stress of anxiety. Alcohol can change mood and behavior, and make it harder to think clearly and move with coordination.

column1: What are the most common consequences of alcoholism?
column2: Drinking a lot can cause irregular heartbeat, high blood pressure or stroke. It can also damage the liver, weaken the immune system and cause cancer. Quitting alcohol is not easy due to the severe withdrawal symptoms.

column1: What do you know about drug addiction?
column2: Drug addiction is a disease that affects the brain and behaviour. Drug addicts can't resist the urge to use certain substances no matter how much harm the drugs may cause.  It isn't just a question of street drugs like it used to be, now many people are addicted to prescription drugs as well. People can become addicted to drugs because they are usually effective in relieving pain and getting rid of reality.

column1: What is compulsive eating and what are its consequences?
column2: Food can also affect brain chemistry in ways that might lead to an addiction. People who compulsively overeat may use food as a way of coping with negative emotions, as a stress reliever or as a means of comfort when they are depressed. As a result, they often feel that their eating is out of control. They think about food all the time and feel guilty, ashamed, or depressed after eating. Compulsive eating is one of the main reasons for obesity.

column1: Are national holidays still important in our globalized world? Why or why not?
column2: National holidays are important as these days have a great significance in the history of a nation. Commemorating these events reminds us of our national heroes and guides different generations about the values and messages associated with these events. They help us promote a sense of nationhood and maintain a sense of unity and cultural belonging. A country's historic time-markers are, for example, gaining independence, sacrifices in war, or the lives and roles of important leaders.

column1: What are the most important public holidays in Hungary and what do they commemorate?
column2: March 15th stands for democracy and freedom and it commemorates the Hungarian Revolution of 1848, which grew into a war for independence from Habsburg rule. August 20th commemorates the foundation of the Hungarian state. It is also called St. Stephen's Day, remembering Stephen I, the first king and founder of the Kingdom of Hungary, who was canonized on August 20th. October 23rd commemorates the revolution of 1956, Hungary's uprising against communism. It also commemorates the Declaration of the Republic, as in 1989, after the fall of communism, Hungary was declared a republic on October 23rd.

column1: How do we celebrate these events?
column2: The main event of the March 15th celebrations is held at the Hungarian National Museum, where young Hungarian revolutionaries held a mass demonstration on the first day of the revolution in 1848. Festivities include ceremonial speeches and a traditional Hussar procession. August 20th festivities start in the morning by raising the Hungarian flag and continue all day long, culminating in a spectacular fireworks display over the Danube. On October 23rd we have a commemoration and memorial ceremony at the '56 Monument at the University of Technology and Economics. Everyone can visit the House of Parliament and the House of Terror Museum free of charge.

column1: Which is the most important religious holiday in your opinion and why?
column2: Our most important religious celebration is Easter, which celebrates the resurrection of Jesus. The many customs and traditions surrounding Easter are about celebrating spring and rebirth. Traditionally, on Easter Monday men visited girls, recited a poem and doused them with buckets of water to ensure they become good wives and bear many children. This tradition has both Christian and pagan roots. In exchange, women would reward their visitors with Easter eggs. Egg painting is one of the oldest traditions associated with Easter. Egg decorating is a form of folk art and it is still part of the Easter celebrations today.

column1: What other public holidays do we have?
column2: We also celebrate Christmas, New Year's Eve and New Year's Day. Pentecost is celebrated seven weeks after Easter, commemorating the descent of the Holy Spirit. November 1st or All Saint's Day is a traditional day for honoring the memory of the dead. It's a custom to light candles and visit the graves of deceased relatives. May 1st or May Day marks Labour Day just like in many other European countries.
